did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs) including ResNet50, DenseNet121, and VGG16 architectures, to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions (differentiating between melanoma and nevus) and evaluate their robustness to image perturbations.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'Original Research' on the first page. It presents original experimental work involving the training of three CNN architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, VGG16) on dermoscopic images, testing their robustness to artificial and natural image perturbations, and evaluating specific mitigation methods (data augmentation, test-time augmentation, anti-aliasing) with reported results and metrics.

title: Robustness of convolutional neural networks in recognition of pigmented skin lesions
brief_summary: This study evaluated the robustness of three convolutional neural network architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, and VGG16) in classifying dermoscopic images of melanoma versus nevus. The models were trained on data from the ISIC, HAM10000, and BCN20000 datasets and tested on artificial perturbations (rotations, zooms, brightness changes) and natural variations from multiple photographs of the same lesions. The researchers assessed the effectiveness of three mitigation strategies: extended data augmentation, test-time augmentation, and anti-aliased network architectures. Results showed that all architectures exhibited brittleness to minor image changes, with the tested methods reducing but not fully eliminating this vulnerability. The study concludes that while robustness can be improved, further research is needed to ensure reliable clinical translation of AI-based skin cancer diagnostic tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. nevus). Images were sourced from ISIC, HAM10000, and BCN20000 datasets.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. nevus). Images were sourced from ISIC, HAM10000, and BCN20000 datasets.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into melanoma or nevus categories
all_task_types.details: The study trained CNN architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, VGG16) to differentiate between dermoscopic melanoma and nevus images. Performance was evaluated using AUROC and P(class change), indicating a binary diagnostic classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images obtained from the ISIC archive (International Skin Imaging Collaboration).

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (ResNet50, DenseNet121, VGG16).
use.testing: A holdout component was used for the artificial test set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models.
Used for training the CNN models.
use.testing: A holdout component was used for the natural test set.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking.

use.testing: An external component was used for the artificial test set.

datasets_used.name: SKINL2
datasets_used.description: Light field image dataset of skin lesions.

use.testing: An external component was used for the artificial test set.

datasets_used.name: PROP

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To obtain robust performance estimates that encompass the stochastic nature of the training process.
To calculate final performance and brittleness metrics (AUROC, P(class change), mean absolute change) by accounting for training variability.

statistical_methods_used.method: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To capture a classifier’s overall performance by summarizing sensitivity and specificity across all possible classification thresholds.
To measure the classifier's susceptibility to change by calculating the probability that the predicted class changes after input image transformation.
To measure by how much on average the model’s output probability changes after the input image is transformed.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance and brittleness were evaluated using descriptive statistics (averages and standard deviations) over five repeated runs, comparing AUROC, P(class change), and mean absolute change across different architectures and mitigation methods without inferential statistical testing.

ISIC.metric: AUROC, P(class change), mean absolute change

DenseNet121.notes: Variations in mean absolute change and P(class change) were slightly lower than ResNet50.

VGG16.notes: Showed the lowest variation out of all three architectures.

ISIC.notes: Used for training. A holdout component was used for the artificial test set. Performance was comparable across 11 artificially transformed test sets.

HAM10000.notes: Used for training. No specific split metrics reported separately from the aggregate baseline performance.

BCN20000.metric: AUROC, P(class change), mean absolute change

ResNet50.notes: Baseline performance AUROC approx 0.9. Natural test set performance was in-between artificial individual and combined transformations.

DenseNet121.notes: Baseline performance AUROC approx 0.9. Natural test set performance was in-between artificial individual and combined transformations.

VGG16.notes: Baseline performance AUROC approx 0.9. Natural test set performance was in-between artificial individual and combined transformations.

BCN20000.notes: Used for training. A holdout component was used for the natural test set.

ResNet50.notes: External component used for artificial test set. Performance metrics averaged across architectures showed VGG16 had best overall performance/brittleness scores.

VGG16.notes: External component used for artificial test set.

Artificial_Test_Set_Holdout.metric: AUROC, P(class change), mean absolute change

VGG16.notes: Best overall performance and brittleness scores among architectures.

Artificial_Test_Set_Holdout.notes: Metrics were always better on the holdout than on the external test set regardless of architecture. Averaged across 12 artificial test sets.

Artificial_Test_Set_External.metric: AUROC, P(class change), mean absolute change

VGG16.notes: Best overall performance and brittleness scores among architectures.

Artificial_Test_Set_External.notes: Metrics were worse on the external test set compared to the holdout test set. Larger improvements in brittleness observed for rotations and zooms than for brightness.

Natural_Test_Set.metric: AUROC, P(class change), mean absolute change

Baseline_Models.notes: Average performance and brittleness was in-between that of the artificial test set with individual transformations and the artificial test set with combined transformations.

BM_plus_TTA.notes: Test-time augmentation always improved both performance and brittleness.

BM_plus_ADA.notes: Results inconsistent; ResNet50 experienced slightly worse brittleness with ADA, DenseNet121 did not.

AAM.notes: Anti-aliased VGG16 suffered an increase in brittleness. Anti-aliasing worked well for ResNet50 and DenseNet121.

Natural_Test_Set.notes: Effectiveness of employed methods was far less pronounced on the natural test set than on either of the artificial test sets. Enhancements were minor.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained weights (implied by use of standard architectures in fastai, but specific source not explicitly detailed in text beyond 'commonly used CNN architectures')

architecture_details.framework: fastai 1.0.61 with torch 1.5.1 and torch vision 0.6.1
architecture_details.classifier_head: fastai’s default custom head (fully connected layers)
architecture_details.anti_aliasing: Anti-aliased architectures (AAM) were created by upgrading downsampling layers (strided convolutions, max-/average-pooling) to include a low-pass filter

training_details.augmentation: Online data augmentation using fastai library presets; Additional Data Augmentation (ADA) involved increased magnitudes of transformations
training_details.runs: Five repeated training and evaluation runs
training_details.test_time_augmentation: Test-time augmentation (TTA) involved rating eight modified duplicates (flip coupled with zoom into all four image corners) and averaging the output

classification_or_output_head.layers: Fully connected layers (fastai default custom head)

preprocessing_techniques: Online data augmentation during training using fastai library presets (type and magnitude adapted from fastai library)
Additional Data Augmentation (ADA) involving increased magnitudes of transformations during training
Test-time augmentation (TTA) involving rating eight modified duplicates (flip coupled with zoom into all four image corners) and averaging the output
Anti-aliased architectures (AAM) created by upgrading downsampling layers (strided convolutions, max-/average-pooling) to include a low-pass filter
Manual sorting of images for the natural test set into similarly looking groups using the four-eyes principle

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training using fastai library presets; type and magnitude adapted from fastai library sensible preset values.
More extreme form of data augmentation during training where the magnitudes of applied transformations were increased.

Applied during inference; model rates eight modified duplicates and averages the output. Duplicates were created using a flip coupled with a zoom into all four image corners. Transformations were deterministic.

conclusions: All tested convolutional neural network architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, and VGG16) exhibited brittleness, meaning their classification of skin lesions was susceptible to minor image perturbations.
Minor image changes that are relatively inconspicuous to human observers can significantly affect the robustness of CNNs differentiating between melanoma and nevus.
The tested mitigation methods (additional data augmentation, test-time augmentation, and anti-aliasing) were able to reduce brittleness to varying degrees while maintaining diagnostic performance.
The reduction in brittleness was more pronounced on artificial test sets than on natural test sets, where the enhancements were minor.
Test-time augmentation consistently improved both performance and brittleness across all architectures.
Additional data augmentation and anti-aliasing showed variable effectiveness depending on the architecture and transformation type, with anti-aliasing increasing brittleness for VGG16.
The observed improvements in robustness were insufficient to fully eliminate the vulnerability of current CNNs to image variations.
Further research is needed to sustain the performance of AI classifiers and facilitate their successful translation into routine clinical practice.
important_remarks: All tested CNN architectures (ResNet50, DenseNet121, VGG16) exhibited brittleness, where minor, inconspicuous image changes significantly affected classification.

Mitigation methods (additional data augmentation, test-time augmentation, anti-aliasing) reduced brittleness but did not fully eliminate it, indicating insufficient robustness for reliable clinical translation.
Improvements in robustness were more pronounced on artificial test sets than on natural test sets, where enhancements were minor.
Test-time augmentation consistently improved both performance and brittleness across all architectures.
Anti-aliasing increased brittleness for VGG16, while it worked well for ResNet50 and DenseNet121.
The natural test set involved subjective manual sorting of images into similar groups using the four-eyes principle, meaning results could vary based on this subjective process.
Some lesions in the natural test set were photographed with extreme zoom differences (>50%), resulting in images with no visual overlap, challenging model consistency.
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests were reported; results rely on descriptive statistics over five repeated runs.
The study highlights that spurious correlations in training data can hamper generalization, a known risk in deep learning for dermatology.

Ethics approval was waived as images were open-source and anonymous, but the reliance on multiple datasets (ISIC, HAM10000, BCN20000, PH2, SKINL2, PROP) introduces potential dataset bias and heterogeneity.
